title: What Grows in the Dark
paragraphs:
In her room at Orrin's, Sister Vess sat on the edge of the bed for a long time without moving.

The fire was lit. The Shepherd had not lied about that. It burned on pale wood that gave off a faint, sweet smell she did not know. The sheets were clean. The jug of water on the side table was full. A single apple had been placed on a pewter plate. The apple was perfect. There was not a mark on it. It should have been impossible, in this wet month, to have so clean an apple.
She did not eat the apple.

She took her knives out one by one and laid them on the bed in a neat row. She took a whetstone and oil from the inner pocket of her coat. She began, slowly and methodically, to hone each edge, the way she had been taught, the way seventeen years had made as natural as breathing.

It was not the knives that needed sharpening. It was her.

A Seeker is trained against many things. She is trained against fear, against sympathy, against the soft pull of exhausted gratitude when a heretic offers her bread. She is trained against the sermons of charismatic men and against the clever arguments of charismatic women. She is trained against loneliness and against the cold.

She is not trained, Sister Vess realised now, against kindness.

The Shepherd was kind. That was the part that had unmade her at the chapel. Every heretic she had taken down in seventeen years had been, at the last, a frightened person with a raised voice or a too-quick hand or a speech ready about the end of the world. They had been easy. They had announced themselves. The Shepherd did not announce anything. He made room for you. He lit your fire. He sent apples. He said, the next one would be too much for you, and meant it.
She sharpened the second knife and thought: I have never in my life met a man who did not, somewhere in him, want something of me.
And she thought: what does it mean that this one does not?
And she thought: or what does it mean that he already has it, and I haven't noticed yet what he took.

She put the third knife down and went to the window.

The rain was still falling. The square was empty. She could see, across the way, the chapel, and the soft yellow light from its windows, and she could hear, faintly, a sound she had not heard before. It was a low, layered hum, as if a great many people were humming the same note very quietly in different rooms of the same house.

She listened for perhaps a minute before she realised she was humming too. Her lips were closed. But the note was moving up out of her chest in time with the note from the chapel.

She put her hand over her mouth. She bit the inside of her cheek hard enough to taste blood.

The humming stopped.

She stood at the window and breathed, and counted her breaths, and after a while her own breath fogged the glass, and she was, for a small and shaky moment, grateful, because it meant she was still making heat, which meant she was still alive, which meant she had not yet been converted.

At the second hour past midnight she went down into the stable.

Her horse was gone.

Orrin had left her a note, in a very neat hand, propped against the empty stall. It said: Gone where you cannot follow. She is well. She is happier here. Rest, sister. Do not try to leave on foot. The marsh is very wide at night.

Sister Vess folded the note into quarters and put it in her pocket. She stood in the empty stall for a long moment, listening to the rain hit the roof.

Then she smiled, privately, to herself, in the dark.

She had not smiled in some years. The muscles of her face had forgotten the shape. It came out crooked.

"Oh," she said, to nobody, "oh, you should not have taken the horse. That was a mistake. That was the first mistake you have made."

She went back up to her room. She ate the apple.

It was perfect. Of course it was perfect. It tasted of apple, and faintly, underneath, of wet wood and of something sweet that had gone bad, and she felt the faint, hot stitch bloom behind her left eye that she had not felt in many years, that she had felt last in a village called Marrow Bridge, in the hour before the intercession.

She sat down at the little table. She opened her report case. She took out a sheet of paper and a pen and, in the small, hard, seventeen-year-trained handwriting of a Crown Seeker, she began to write her first letter of the assignment.

She wrote:

To the Prefect of Graywater.

I have arrived at Aldmoor. The situation is worse than the report indicated. The infection is not in one man. It is in the soil, and in the rain, and in the walls of the chapel, and, as of one hour ago, in me.

I will require, at minimum, a full interdiction company. I will require fire. I will require a priest who has not set foot in the Graywater prefecture in the last year, and who does not love kindness, and who can be trusted to refuse even the cleanest apple.

I have seven days.

I do not know, when the Shepherd walks into this room on the seventh morning, whether I will still be the woman writing this letter. So I will be brief, and honest, and I will tell you, Prefect, the one thing that matters, which is this:

He is not a man. He was, perhaps, once. He is not, now. I have met ten heretics who believed themselves to be gods and I intercede-d them, all of them, because they were wrong. This one is not wrong.

Burn the village. Burn the marsh. Burn, if you must, me.

Yours in service,
Sister Vess.

She sealed the letter. She did not send it. There was nobody to send it with.

She set it on the little table, propped against the empty pewter plate where the apple had been.

Then Sister Vess blew out the candle, and lay down on the clean sheets in her boots and her coat with all three knives within reach of her right hand, and waited for morning, or for whichever came first.

Outside, the rain fell.

In the square, very quietly, something began, for the first time in thirty-two days, to grow.
